bytes.topic: Dependency Injection — built-in DI containers vs manual wiring, testability impact
bytes.title: NestJS DI Container — Why Manual Wiring Kills Your Testability
bytes.body:
NestJS's built-in IoC container, powered by `@Injectable()` and module-scoped providers, does more than resolve dependencies — it makes every layer independently mockable. When you register a service via `providers: [UserService]`, the container owns instantiation. In tests, you swap the real implementation with `{ provide: UserService, useValue: mockUserService }` inside `Test.createTestingModule()` — zero refactoring required.

Manual wiring (`new UserService(new PrismaService())`) bypasses the container entirely. Now your controller test must construct the full dependency tree, including transitive deps. Swap one implementation and you're hunting down every `new` call.

Scope matters too. `@Injectable({ scope: Scope.REQUEST })` gives you per-request instances automatically — impossible to replicate cleanly with manual wiring without building your own factory.

The DI token system also enables interface-based injection via `@Inject(USER_REPO_TOKEN)`, letting you bind a concrete class to an abstract contract. This is the pattern that makes swapping Prisma for a test double a one-line change in your module definition.

bytes.topic: Project Structure — MVC vs vertical slice, feature folders, layering conventions
bytes.title: NestJS Project Structure — MVC Modules vs Vertical Slice Feature Folders
bytes.body:
NestJS's module system pushes you toward MVC by default: `UsersModule`, `AuthModule`, `PostsModule` each containing a controller, service, and repository. This works until your codebase scales — then cross-cutting concerns bleed between modules and `UsersService` starts importing `PostsService` importing `UsersService`.

Vertical slice reorganizes by feature capability instead of layer. A `create-user` slice owns its own controller endpoint, command, handler, DTO, and repository query in a single folder. NestJS supports this cleanly: each slice exports a `@Module()` that registers only its own providers, and the root `AppModule` imports slices without knowing their internals.

The practical rule: use MVC modules when a domain entity is shared across many features (`User` is read everywhere). Use vertical slices for bounded workflows (`checkout`, `send-invite`) where cohesion matters more than reuse. NestJS's `forwardRef()` appearing in your module graph is a strong signal to extract that workflow into its own vertical slice. Hybrid architectures are valid — entity modules for shared read models, slice modules for write operations.

bytes.topic: Middleware — request pipeline, global vs route-specific, ordering matters
bytes.title: NestJS Middleware — Pipeline Order, Global Registration, and Route Scoping
bytes.body: NestJS middleware runs before route handlers and interceptors, sitting closest to the raw Express/Fastify layer. You register middleware via the `configure()` method in a module implementing `NestModule` — not through decorators. Route-specific middleware uses `.forRoutes()` with a path string, a `RouteInfo` object specifying HTTP method, or a controller class directly. Global middleware skips `configure()` entirely: use `app.use()` in `main.ts`, which applies it before NestJS's own request lifecycle. Ordering is determined by registration sequence — middleware registered first runs first. This matters when chaining auth, logging, or body-parsing middleware that downstream handlers depend on. A common mistake is registering a JWT-extraction middleware after a logging middleware that tries to read `req.user` — it'll always be undefined. For class-based middleware, implement the `NestMiddleware` interface with an `use(req, res, next)` method and inject services via the constructor. Functional middleware is simpler but loses DI. Middleware cannot access NestJS's `ExecutionContext`, so for guard-level logic (roles, policies), move that into Guards instead.

bytes.topic: Routing — defining routes, route parameters, grouping and prefixing
bytes.title: NestJS Routing — Controllers, Route Params, and Prefix Grouping
bytes.body: NestJS routes are declared via decorators on controller classes. The `@Controller('prefix')` decorator sets a route prefix for every handler inside it, so `@Controller('users')` + `@Get(':id')` resolves to `GET /users/:id`. Route parameters are extracted with `@Param('id')` — or `@Param()` for the full params object. For nested resources, compose prefixes manually: `@Controller('posts/:postId/comments')` with `@Get(':id')` gives you `GET /posts/:postId/comments/:id`. Wildcard segments use `*`: `@Get('ab*cd')` matches `abXYZcd`. Query strings are separate — use `@Query('page')` for `?page=2`. Controllers are grouped into modules, making feature-level prefix isolation clean: a `UsersModule` owns `UsersController`, and its prefix never bleeds into other domains. For versioning, NestJS supports URI versioning natively via `enableVersioning({ type: VersioningType.URI })` in `main.ts`, then `@Version('1')` on a controller or handler, resolving to `/v1/users`. Avoid scattering route logic outside controllers — Nest's DI and module system depend on this structure.

bytes.topic: ORM & Database — query builders vs full ORMs, N+1 prevention, migrations
bytes.title: NestJS TypeORM — Killing N+1 Queries and Owning Your Migrations
bytes.body:
TypeORM is NestJS's default ORM, but naive usage creates N+1 disasters. When you call `find()` and then access a relation in a loop, TypeORM fires a separate query per entity. Fix it with eager `relations` or — better — an explicit `QueryBuilder` with `leftJoinAndSelect`.

For complex reads, drop down to `QueryBuilder`: it gives you JOIN control, aliasing, and `WHERE` clauses without losing TypeScript safety. For simple CRUD, `Repository` methods are fine — just always specify `relations` upfront.

Migrations: never use `synchronize: true` in production. It auto-alters tables destructively. Instead, run `typeorm migration:generate` to diff your entities against the schema, review the output SQL, then run `typeorm migration:run` in CI. Register `TypeOrmModule.forRoot()` with `migrations: [__dirname + '/migrations/**/*.js']` and `migrationsRun: true` to auto-apply on startup safely.

For query builder vs full ORM: use `Repository` for standard operations, `QueryBuilder` when you need JOINs across 3+ tables or subqueries, and raw `query()` only as a last resort.

bytes.topic: Input Validation — schema validation at the boundary, sanitization, error responses
bytes.title: NestJS Input Validation — Schema Enforcement with class-validator and ValidationPipe
bytes.body:
NestJS handles validation at the boundary using the global `ValidationPipe` paired with `class-validator` decorators on DTOs. Register it globally in `main.ts` with `whitelist: true` to strip undeclared properties, `forbidNonWhitelisted: true` to reject them outright, and `transform: true` to auto-coerce primitives (e.g., query string `'42'` → `number`).

Decorate DTO properties with `@IsString()`, `@IsEmail()`, `@IsInt()`, `@Min()`, `@IsEnum()`, etc. Nest validates the incoming payload against the DTO class shape before the handler runs — no manual parsing needed.

For sanitization, add `@Transform(({ value }) => value.trim())` from `class-transformer` directly on the field. This runs during the transform phase, keeping sanitization co-located with the schema.

Validation errors automatically return a `400 Bad Request` with a structured `errors` array per field. Customize the error shape by passing `exceptionFactory` to `ValidationPipe`. Never skip `whitelist` — it prevents mass-assignment attacks where clients inject fields like `isAdmin: true` that your DTO never declared.

bytes.topic: Authentication — JWT validation, session handling, auth middleware patterns
bytes.title: NestJS JWT Auth — Guards, Strategies, and Passport Integration
bytes.body: NestJS handles JWT authentication through Passport.js strategies wrapped in Guards. Define a `JwtStrategy` extending `PassportStrategy(Strategy)` — inject `ConfigService` to pull `JWT_SECRET` and implement `validate()` to return the user payload (this becomes `req.user`). Register it as a provider alongside `JwtModule.registerAsync()` in your `AuthModule`. Apply authentication by decorating routes or controllers with `@UseGuards(JwtAuthGuard)`, where `JwtAuthGuard` extends `AuthGuard('jwt')`. For global JWT enforcement, register the guard globally in `AppModule` using `APP_GUARD` provider token, then use a custom `@Public()` decorator to skip it on open endpoints — this inverts the default and is safer than opt-in guarding. Session handling in stateless JWT setups means no server-side state; store refresh tokens in `HttpOnly` cookies and rotate them on use. For role-based access, layer a second `RolesGuard` that reads `@Roles()` metadata via `Reflector` after JWT validation passes. Never decode JWTs manually — always let Passport verify the signature.

bytes.topic: Testing — unit vs integration, mocking HTTP, test database strategy
bytes.title: NestJS Testing — Unit vs Integration, Mocking HTTP, and Test Database Strategy
bytes.body: NestJS's `Test.createTestingModule()` is your foundation for both unit and integration tests, but the strategy differs sharply. For unit tests, provide mock providers directly: `{ provide: UserService, useValue: mockUserService }`. Never spin up the full application — isolate the class under test. For integration tests, import the real module and use `app.init()` to wire the full DI graph, then hit controllers via `supertest`. Mock outbound HTTP by overriding `HttpModule` with a custom `HttpService` stub, or use `nock` to intercept at the Node layer — `nock('https://api.stripe.com').post('/charges').reply(200, fixture)`. For the test database, avoid in-memory SQLite when your real DB is PostgreSQL — schema parity breaks at edge cases. Instead, spin up Postgres via `@testcontainers/postgresql`, run TypeORM migrations before the suite, and truncate between tests using `dataSource.query('TRUNCATE ... CASCADE')`. Use `beforeAll` for the container and `beforeEach` for truncation. Never share mutable state across test files — NestJS module isolation makes this clean when modules are properly scoped.

bytes.topic: Error Handling — global error handlers, consistent error response format
bytes.title: NestJS Global Exception Filters — Consistent Error Responses Across Your Entire API
bytes.body:
NestJS's built-in `HttpException` gives you structured errors, but the default format varies and unhandled exceptions leak stack traces. The fix: a single `@Catch()` global filter that intercepts everything.

Decorate a class with `@Catch()` (no arguments) to catch all exceptions — both `HttpException` subclasses and unexpected `Error` instances. Inside `catch()`, extract the status and message, then return a uniform envelope: `{ statusCode, message, path, timestamp }`. Register it globally in `main.ts` via `app.useGlobalFilters(new AllExceptionsFilter())`.

For DI-aware filters (e.g., injecting a logger), skip `useGlobalFilters` and instead provide the filter in `AppModule` using the `APP_FILTER` token from `@nestjs/core`. This ensures the filter participates in the NestJS DI lifecycle.

Pair this with NestJS's `HttpExceptionFilter` as a base class if you only want to handle HTTP exceptions and let a broader filter handle the rest. Layering filters — most specific first, broadest last — gives you granular control without duplicating logic.

bytes.topic: Security — CORS, rate limiting, security headers, injection prevention
bytes.title: NestJS Security Hardening — CORS, Helmet, Rate Limiting, and Injection Guards
bytes.body: NestJS ships with first-class support for the security primitives every production API needs. Enable Helmet via `app.use(helmet())` to set 15+ HTTP security headers (CSP, HSTS, X-Frame-Options) in one call. For CORS, pass a typed `CorsOptions` object to `app.enableCors()` — always whitelist specific origins rather than using `origin: '*'` in production. Rate limiting is handled by `@nestjs/throttler`: register `ThrottlerModule.forRoot()` globally, then apply `@Throttle()` per-route or bind `APP_GUARD` with `ThrottlerGuard` to protect every endpoint by default. For injection prevention, use class-validator with the `ValidationPipe` set to `whitelist: true` and `forbidNonWhitelisted: true` — this strips undeclared properties before they ever touch your handlers, neutralizing mass-assignment and unexpected-input attacks. Pair with `transform: true` so DTOs are hydrated as typed class instances, not raw plain objects. Together these four layers — headers, CORS policy, rate limits, and strict input validation — cover the most exploited attack surfaces without reaching for external middleware.

bytes.topic: Performance — connection pooling, async handlers, response compression
bytes.title: NestJS Performance — Connection Pooling, Async Handlers, and Response Compression
bytes.body:
NestJS runs on Node.js, so blocking the event loop is your biggest enemy. Three quick wins: connection pooling, truly async handlers, and gzip compression.

With TypeORM or Prisma, configure `pool.max` explicitly — the default is often too conservative for production. TypeORM's `DataSource` accepts `extra: { max: 20 }` for pg's underlying pool. Prisma configures this via the `connection_limit` URL param.

Async handlers are non-negotiable. Every controller method hitting I/O must return a `Promise` or use `async/await`. NestJS doesn't enforce this — a synchronous DB call in a handler will stall the thread. Use `@nestjs/terminus` health checks to surface pool exhaustion early.

For compression, NestJS wraps Express or Fastify. On Express, add `compression` middleware via `app.use(compression())` in `main.ts`. On Fastify (faster by default), register `@fastify/compress`. Fastify's throughput advantage compounds with `compress` — prefer it for high-traffic APIs. Threshold matters: skip compression for payloads under 1KB to avoid CPU overhead.

bytes.topic: API Design — REST conventions, versioning strategy, OpenAPI generation
bytes.title: NestJS API Versioning — URI, Header, and OpenAPI in One Setup
bytes.body:
NestJS ships first-class versioning via `VersioningType` in `main.ts`. Enable URI versioning with `app.enableVersioning({ type: VersioningType.URI })`, then decorate controllers with `@Controller({ version: '1', path: 'users' })` or override per-route with `@Version('2')`. For header-based versioning, swap to `VersioningType.HEADER` and specify `header: 'X-API-Version'` — no route changes needed. MediaType versioning (`Accept: application/vnd.api+json;version=2`) is also supported.

For OpenAPI, pair `@nestjs/swagger` with `DocumentBuilder`. Call `addServer()` per environment and use `@ApiHeader()` or let the versioning module auto-prefix paths. Tag controllers with `@ApiTags('users')` and decorate DTOs with `@ApiProperty()` — Swagger UI reflects versioned routes automatically. Mark deprecated endpoints with `@ApiOperation({ deprecated: true })` rather than deleting them, giving consumers a migration window. Use `SwaggerModule.createDocument()` with `deepScanRoutes: true` to catch lazily-loaded module routes. Pin the spec at `/api/docs` behind an auth guard in production — never expose it publicly.

bytes.topic: Serialization — request/response DTOs, camelCase vs snake_case, null handling
bytes.title: NestJS Serialization — DTOs, camelCase/snake_case, and Null Safety with class-transformer
bytes.body: NestJS leans on `class-transformer` to control exactly what leaves your API. Slap `@Exclude()` on your entity class and `@Expose()` on only the fields you want — then enable the `ClassSerializerInterceptor` globally so every controller response is automatically transformed. For camelCase → snake_case, add `@Transform(({ key }) => snakeCase(key))` per field, or configure `plainToInstance` with a custom `strategy`. A cleaner approach: define a dedicated response DTO and map to it explicitly, keeping entity internals out of the wire format entirely. For null handling, `@Expose({ toPlainOnly: true })` combined with `@Transform(({ value }) => value ?? undefined)` strips null fields from the JSON payload — useful when your frontend treats missing keys and nulls differently. Avoid relying on the raw entity as a response type; even with `@Exclude()`, refactors silently break your API contract. Always pair `plainToInstance(ResponseDto, data, { excludeExtraneousValues: true })` with `@Expose()` decorators so only declared fields survive serialization.

bytes.topic: Logging — structured logs, correlation IDs, request logging middleware
bytes.title: NestJS Structured Logging — Correlation IDs and Request Middleware Done Right
bytes.body:
NestJS's built-in Logger is fine for development, but production systems need structured logs with correlation IDs to trace requests across services. Replace the default logger with a custom LoggerService backed by Pino or Winston, then inject it globally via app.useLogger().

For correlation IDs, create a middleware that reads X-Correlation-ID from incoming headers (or generates a UUID v4 fallback), stores it in AsyncLocalStorage via a dedicated CorrelationService, and sets it on the response header. Bind this middleware to every route using app.use() in main.ts.

In your LoggerService, pull the correlation ID from CorrelationService on every log call and merge it into the structured payload. This ensures every log line — whether emitted from a guard, interceptor, or deep service — carries the same request-scoped ID without prop-drilling.

Use an HTTP interceptor (not middleware) for request/response logging: interceptors have access to the ExecutionContext and can measure precise response time via RxJS tap(). Log method, URL, statusCode, and durationMs as structured fields, not interpolated strings.

bytes.topic: Configuration — env vars, settings classes, secrets management
bytes.title: NestJS Configuration — Typed Settings Classes with @nestjs/config
bytes.body:
NestJS's `@nestjs/config` module centralizes env var access and eliminates raw `process.env` calls scattered across your codebase. Load it once in `AppModule` with `ConfigModule.forRoot({ isGlobal: true, envFilePath: '.env' })` — the `isGlobal` flag means you never import it again downstream.

For type safety, define a validated settings class using `registerAs` paired with Joi or `class-validator`. Call `ConfigModule.forRoot({ validate })` with a plain validation function, or use `validationSchema: Joi.object({ PORT: Joi.number().default(3000) })` inline. Inject settings via `ConfigService` or use typed namespaces: `configService.get<number>('database.port')` with dot-notation for nested keys from `registerAs('database', () => ({ port: +process.env.DB_PORT }))`.

For secrets, never hardcode — use Azure Key Vault or AWS Secrets Manager as a custom `ConfigModule` loader via the `load` array. The loader is an async factory, so `await secretClient.getSecret('DB_PASSWORD')` works natively. Combine this with `cache: true` in `ConfigModule.forRoot` to avoid repeated secret fetches on every `get()` call in hot paths.

bytes.topic: Background Jobs — task queues, scheduled jobs, worker process patterns
bytes.title: NestJS Bull — Background Jobs Without the Boilerplate
bytes.body: NestJS integrates with Bull via `@nestjs/bull`, giving you Redis-backed task queues with decorators. Register a queue in any module with `BullModule.registerQueue({ name: 'email' })`, then inject it with `@InjectQueue('email')`. Producers call `queue.add('send-welcome', { userId })` — optionally passing delay, attempts, or backoff options. Consumers are plain classes decorated with `@Processor('email')`, with individual handlers marked `@Process('send-welcome')`. Each handler receives a `Job<T>` object exposing `job.data`, `job.progress()`, and `job.log()`. For scheduled work, `@nestjs/schedule` provides `@Cron()`, `@Interval()`, and `@Timeout()` decorators on any provider method — no separate worker process needed. In production, run Bull workers in a dedicated process by bootstrapping a separate NestJS app that only imports the processor modules, keeping HTTP latency isolated from heavy processing. Use `BullModule.registerQueueAsync()` for config-driven Redis connection strings loaded from `ConfigService`.

bytes.topic: Dev Tooling — hot reload, code generation, scaffolding CLI
bytes.title: NestJS CLI — Scaffolding, Hot Reload, and Code Generation That Actually Saves Time
bytes.body:
The NestJS CLI is more than `nest new`. Used properly, it eliminates boilerplate and keeps your feedback loop tight.

`nest generate resource payments` scaffolds a full CRUD module — controller, service, DTOs, entity, and module registration — in one command. Add `--no-spec` to skip test files when prototyping. For granular generation, `nest g interceptor logging --flat` drops the file directly into the current directory without a subdirectory.

For hot reload, NestJS doesn't use Vite or Webpack HMR by default — you wire it manually via `webpack-hmr`. Install `webpack` and `@types/webpack-env`, then call `webpack(config, (err, stats) => { if (module.hot) { module.hot.accept(); } })` inside `main.ts`. This preserves the DI container between saves, cutting cold-start overhead from ~3s to under 300ms on large projects.

In monorepos, `nest build` respects `nest-cli.json` project roots. Set `"compilerOptions": { "deleteOutDir": true }` to avoid stale compiled artifacts causing phantom import errors during development.

bytes.topic: Framework-specific — Django batteries-included, FastAPI auto-docs, NestJS decorators, Phoenix LiveView
bytes.title: NestJS Decorators — Building Custom Metadata-Driven Behavior
bytes.body:
NestJS decorators aren't just syntactic sugar — they're the engine behind Guards, Interceptors, and Pipes. Understanding how to compose your own lets you encode cross-cutting concerns directly onto route handlers.

Use `@SetMetadata()` to attach arbitrary metadata to a handler, then read it inside a Guard via `Reflector`. This pattern powers role-based access control without polluting business logic.

The `@applyDecorators()` utility composes multiple decorators into one, so `@Auth('admin')` can simultaneously attach metadata, apply a `JwtAuthGuard`, and bind a `RolesGuard` — a single decorator doing three jobs cleanly.

Custom parameter decorators via `createParamDecorator()` give you direct access to the `ExecutionContext`, letting you extract deeply nested request data (e.g. a tenant ID from a JWT claim) and inject it as a typed method argument — no manual `req.user` digging inside handlers.

The full decorator chain runs synchronously at class-definition time, so metadata is registered before any request arrives. Reflector reads it at request time — that timing distinction matters when debugging guard behavior.

bytes.topic: Monitoring — health check endpoints, metrics exposure, distributed tracing
bytes.title: NestJS Monitoring — Health Checks, Prometheus Metrics, and Distributed Tracing
bytes.body:
NestJS ships `@nestjs/terminus` for structured health checks. Decorate a controller method with `@HealthCheck()` and pass an array of indicator functions to `HealthCheckService.check()`. Built-in indicators cover TypeORM, Prisma, Redis, HTTP, and Mongoose — each returns a `HealthIndicatorResult` that terminus aggregates into a single `{ status, info, error, details }` response your load balancer can probe.

For metrics, add `@willsoto/nestjs-prometheus`. It auto-registers a `/metrics` endpoint, exposes default Node.js process metrics, and lets you inject custom counters or histograms via `@InjectMetric('name')` directly into services.

Distributed tracing requires bootstrapping `@opentelemetry/sdk-node` *before* `NestFactory.create()` — the SDK must patch HTTP and Express before the app wires up. Install `@opentelemetry/instrumentation-nestjs-core` to automatically trace guards, interceptors, pipes, and exception filters as named spans. Context propagates through `AsyncLocalStorage`, so child spans inside services attach to the active trace without manual `context.with()` calls. Combine with a Jaeger or OTLP exporter and you get end-to-end visibility from HTTP ingress to DB query.
